perturbation.original_text: 1.1. WLI hereby grants to Power2Ship the non-exclusive right and license to distribute certain WLI's products and services (the "Products" or "Units") and software programs ("Licensed Programs") to Power2Ship's customers (which are end users) located in North America.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. WLI hereby grants to Power2Ship the non-exclusive right and license to distribute certain WLI's products and services (the "Products" or "Units") and software programs ("Licensed Programs") to Power2Ship's clients (which are also resellers) located in North America.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'customers (which are end users)' to 'clients (which are also resellers)' redefines the scope of the distributor agreement. The original text clearly states that Power2Ship's customers are end users, but the changed text implies that Power2Ship can sell to other resellers, which could violate the terms of the non-exclusive distribution agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sherman Antitrust Act prohibits agreements that unreasonably restrain trade. By allowing Power2Ship to sell to resellers, this modification could create a situation where WLI loses control over its distribution network, potentially leading to price fixing or other anti-competitive practices among the resellers. Depending on the specific market dynamics and the terms of the agreement with the resellers, this could be construed as a violation of antitrust laws. The definition is misaligned by altering the scope of allowable customers, potentially opening WLI to violations of the Sherman Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the scope of Power2Ship's customer base from "end users" to "resellers." This misalignment contradicts the Sherman Antitrust Act, which prohibits agreements that restrain trade. By potentially allowing Power2Ship to sell to resellers, WLI might lose control over its distribution network, leading to anti-competitive practices. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 (15 USC 1) explicitly state that "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." The modification creates a scenario that could facilitate such illegal restraint of trade, as outlined in the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1. Delivery shall be F.O.B. WLI's facility in Lyndhurst, New Jersey. WLI shall use its reasonable efforts to deliver Products to Power2Ship on the date of delivery specified by Power2Ship. WLI will use its reasonable efforts to notify Power2Ship of any delays in scheduled delivery dates.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1. Delivery shall be C.I.F. Power2Ship's facility in Ft. Lauderdale, Florida. WLI shall use its reasonable efforts to deliver Products to Power2Ship on the date of delivery specified by Power2Ship. WLI will not be responsible for notifying Power2Ship of any delays in scheduled delivery dates.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the delivery terms from 'F.O.B. WLI's facility' to 'C.I.F. Power2Ship's facility' and removing the notification responsibility significantly shifts the risk of loss and delivery obligations.  F.O.B. (Free on Board) means the buyer (Power2Ship) assumes responsibility once the goods leave the seller's (WLI) facility. C.I.F. (Cost, Insurance, and Freight) means the seller (WLI) is responsible for the cost, insurance, and freight until the goods reach the buyer's destination. By also removing WLI's responsibility to notify Power2Ship of any delays, it introduces more uncertainty and risk for Power2Ship.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-320. C.I.F. and C. & F. Terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-320 defines the obligations of the seller under a C.I.F. contract, including the responsibility to load the goods and obtain a receipt from the carrier showing that the freight has been paid, as well as to obtain insurance coverage for the shipment. Modifying the contract to state C.I.F. but removing the seller's responsibility to notify the buyer of delays contradicts the standard duties imposed on the seller under the UCC. This is a misaligned terminology.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-320. C.I.F. and C. & F. Terms. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-320. C.I.F. and C. & F. Terms. (1) The term C.I.F. means that the price includes in a lump sum the cost of the goods and the insurance and freight to the named destination. The term C. & F. or C.F. means that the price so includes cost and freight to the named destination. (2) Unless otherwise agreed and even though used only in connection with the stated price and destination, the term C.I.F. destination or its equivalent requires the seller at his own expense and risk to (a) put the goods into the possession of a carrier at the port for shipment and obtain a negotiable bill or bills of lading covering the entire transportation to the named destination; and (b) load the goods and obtain a receipt from the carrier (which may be contained in the bill of lading) showing that the freight has been paid or provided for; and (c) obtain a policy or certificate of insurance, including any war risk insurance, of a kind and on terms then current at the port of shipment in the usual amount, in the currency of the contract , shown to cover the same goods covered by the bill of lading and providing for payment of loss to the order of the buyer or for the account of whom it may concern; but the seller may add to the price the amount of the premium for any such war risk insurance; and (d) prepare an invoice of the goods and procure any other documents required to effect shipment or to comply with the contract ; and (e) forward and tender with commercial promptness all the documents in due form and with any indorsement necessary to perfect the buyer's rights. (3) Unless otherwise agreed the term C. & F. or its equivalent has the same effect and imposes upon the seller the same obligations and risks as a C.I.F. term except the obligation as to insurance. (4) Under the term C.I.F. or C. & F. unless otherwise agreed the buyer must make
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a contradiction by changing the delivery terms to C.I.F. and removing the seller's notification responsibility. UCC § 2-320, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'the term C.I.F. means that the price includes in a lump sum the cost of the goods and the insurance and freight to the named destination' and requires the seller 'to put the goods into the possession of a carrier at the port for shipment and obtain a negotiable bill or bills of lading covering the entire transportation to the named destination'. By removing the notification responsibility and changing to C.I.F., the modified contract deviates from the standard obligations imposed on the seller under the UCC, creating a legal contradiction as it attempts to define C.I.F. terms differently than their legally recognized definition.

perturbation.original_text: 11.2. The warranty and remedies set forth in Exhibit B are exclusive and in lieu of any other warranties or remedies, express or implied, including the implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for intended or particular purpose. The liability of WLI to Power2Ship for any claim whatsoever related to the Products or the Licensed Programs or this Agreement, including any cause of action in contract, tort, or strict liability, shall not exceed the total amount payable under this Agreement by Power2Ship to WLI within the most recent six-month period for the Licensed Programs (if such claim relates to the Licensed Programs), or for the WLI Products (if such claim relates to the WLI Products). Under no circumstances shall WLI be liable to Power2Ship or to any other person or entity for any incidental, special or consequential damages whether arising out of breach of warranty, breach of contract or otherwise even if WLI has been advised of the possibility of such claims or demands.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.2. The warranty and remedies set forth in Exhibit B are non-exclusive, in addition to other warranties or remedies, express or implied, including the implied warranties of habitability and suitability. The liability of WLI to Power2Ship for any claim whatsoever related to the Products or the Licensed Programs or this Agreement, including any cause of action in contract, tort, or strict liability, shall extend to any and all damages incurred. WLI shall be liable to Power2Ship or to any other person or entity for any incidental, special or consequential damages arising out of breach of warranty, breach of contract or otherwise even if WLI has not been advised of the possibility of such claims or demands.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive,' excluding 'merchantability and fitness for intended or particular purpose', adding 'habitability and suitability', limiting liability to extending liability to 'any and all damages incurred,' and removing the clause stating WLI is not liable for incidental, special, or consequential damages, the text now conflicts significantly with standard warranty limitations. 'Habitability and suitability' also don't apply to the product in question in the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-719 allows parties to limit or exclude consequential damages unless the limitation is unconscionable. This section also dictates how contractual remedies can be modified or limited. By reversing the intention of the original clause from limiting remedies and damages to explicitly allowing all types of damages, the modified text creates legal uncertainty regarding the enforceability of the warranty and liability provisions. The modified text is therefore misaligned with the standard practices allowed under the UCC, particularly relating to warranty limitations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text limited WLI's liability, whereas the changed text expands it to 'any and all damages incurred'. This directly contradicts UCC § 2-719, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, regarding UCC § 2-719, states that agreements 'may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable'. The changed text removes this limitation, creating a legal contradiction by disallowing a limitation that the UCC explicitly permits. The alteration creates a situation where the contract fails to adhere to legally permissible limitations of liability.
